Summary: Sox9 is a key transcriptional regulator that specifies astrocytes during development. Sox9 is specifically expressed in naïve astrocytes and is upregulated in reactive astrocytes after brain and spinal cord injury. In naïve animals, Sox9 maintains basal Hk1 levels. Nerve injury triggers Sox9 phosphorylation that enhances nuclear translocation and increases Sox9 transcriptional activity. Sox9 phosphorylation leads to pathological Hk1 upregulation, promotes aberrant glycolytic flux, contributes to the emergence of an Hk1-mediated neuroinflammatory astrocyte cluster, and induces an Astro 2 subtype with neurodegenerative and demyelinating properties. Sox9 phosphorylation and its downstream effects (pathological Hk1 upregulation, aberrant glycolytic flux, and emergence of neuroinflammatory/Astro 2 astrocyte subtypes) may be involved in pain pathogenesis.
References: Chen, Y., Liao, Y., Zhu, H., Wu, Z., Wan, Y., Feng, X., Fan, C., Zhan, D., Cai, H., Cheung, M., Cheung, C. W., & Liu, J. A. (2025). Sox9 regulation of hexokinase 1 controls neuroinflammatory astrocyte subtypes in a rat model of neuropathic pain. Nat Commun, 16(1), 10249. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-65092-5
